panels.description: Wide angle shot: Kala Nag, a massive Indian elephant, uses his trunk to lift a terrified Little Toomai into the air. The boy hangs level with the forehead of another elephant, Pudmini. Standing in front of them is Petersen Sahib, a distinguished European man in colonial safari gear. The background is a bustling elephant-catching camp in the jungle.

Medium shot: Big Toomai stands on the dusty ground, looking up with a face full of intense pride and respect. He gestures toward his son in the air with a calloused hand. His expression is earnest and filled with paternal joy.

bubbles.text: He is Toomai, my son, Sahib.

panels.description: Medium close shot: Petersen Sahib looking down with a patronizing, kind smile. He is framed against the dense green foliage of the Assam forest. In the blurred background, other elephant-catchers are seen laughing at his joke. Bright, direct sunlight.

bubbles.text: When thou hast seen the elephants dance, and then I will let thee go into all the Keddahs.
Remember, though, that Keddahs are not good for children to play in.
There was another roar of laughter, for that is an old joke among elephant-catchers, and it means just never.

panels.description: Medium shot: Little Toomai, now back on the ground, looks up at Big Toomai with wide, curious eyes. Big Toomai looks stern and dismissive, looking out toward the horizon. The dark shadows of the forest edge loom behind them.

bubbles.text: And when didst thou see the elephants dance?
That thou shouldst never be one of these hill buffaloes of trackers.

panels.description: Wide angle shot: A majestic line of grunting elephants marches down a narrow hill path toward the plains. Kala Nag is prominent, carrying Big Toomai, Little Toomai, and his mother nursing a baby brother. The vast landscape of the Indian plains opens up in the distance.

bubbles.text: The line of grunting, squealing elephants rolled down the hill path to the plains.

panels.description: Medium shot: At a river ford, the march is chaotic. Kala Nag hits a smaller, unruly elephant in the ribs. On Kala Nag's back, Little Toomai leans close to his father to speak. Big Toomai grunts, focusing on driving the elephant through the water. Mud and water splash in the foreground.

bubbles.text: What did Petersen Sahib mean by the elephant dance?
We have swept the hills of wild elephants at the last catch.
That thou shouldst never be one of these hill buffaloes of trackers.
